messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_doc_06f8fa1cac644fe2.pdf
Equipment class: chiller

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_505c865df8fdebc3", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Cooling tower piping water velocity", "summary": "Piping is sized to yield water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s].", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Cooling tower piping water velocity", "title": "Cooling tower piping water velocity", "summary": "Piping is sized to yield water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s]."}, "evidence": "98⏐ COMPONENTS
Design of piping from the tower sump to the pump should be
undertaken with caution. Place the sump level above the top of the
pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction
strainers should be equipped with inlet and outlet gauges that indi-
cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield
water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s]. Use fric-
tion factors for aged or old pipes.
In cooling tower installations, ascertain that sufficient NPSH
(net positive suction head—see Section 5.5.2) for the condenser-
water pump is provided, and design  the connections so that the flow
through each cooling tower has th e same pressure drop. Install
equalizing lines of proper size between towers; otherwise, one
tower will fill and the other one will overflow.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_47e656a8248b50e5", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Evaporative condenser water flow velocity", "summary": "Piping should be sized with flow velocities of 5 to 10 fps [1.5 to 3.0 m/s].", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Evaporative condenser water flow velocity", "title": "Evaporative condenser water flow velocity", "summary": "Piping should be sized with flow velocities of 5 to 10 fps [1.5 to 3.0 m/s]."}, "evidence": "In such applications, they should be located as close as possible
to the refrigeration machinery to keep the refrigerant lines short. In
the winter, the water can often be shut off with the dry coil provid-
ing adequate heat rejection. Evaporative condensers are also used to
cool recirculating water in closed-loop water-source heat pump sys-
tems (see Chapter 7).
Figure 5-8 shows two evaporative condenser types using city,
well, or river makeup water. Piping should be sized in accordance
with the principles outlined in Section 5.4.5 with flow velocities of
5 to 10 fps [1.5 to 3.0 m/s]. Where city water is used, a pump is
usually not required since the water arrives under pressure. For
well or river water, pumps may be necessary, in which case the.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_c1e3fd893e393917", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Evaporative condenser makeup water rate", "summary": "Makeup water approximately 1.6 to 2.0 gph/ton [0.5 to 0.6 mL/s per kW] by evaporation plus 0.5 to 0.6 gph/ton [0.15 to 0.18 mL/s per kW] for bleed.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "Evaporative condenser makeup water rate", "title": "Evaporative condenser makeup water rate", "summary": "Makeup water approximately 1.6 to 2.0 gph/ton [0.5 to 0.6 mL/s per kW] by evaporation plus 0.5 to 0.6 gph/ton [0.15 to 0.18 mL/s per kW] for bleed."}, "evidence": "11.1 PURPOSE OF THIS MANUAL
This manual was prepared to assist entry-level engineers in the
design of air-conditioning systems. It is also usable—in conjunc-
tion with fundamental HV AC&R resource materials—as a senior-
or graduate-level text for a university course in HV AC system
design. This manual was intended to fill the void between theory
and practice, to bridge the gap be tween real-world design practices
and the theoretical knowledge acquir ed in the typical college course
or textbook. Courses and texts usually concentrate on theoretical
calculations and analytical procedures or they focus upon the
design of components.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_32d3c95cd720234d", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cooling tower sump and pump piping design", "summary": "Place sump level above pump casing top for positive prime; pitch piping up to tower or pump suction to eliminate air pockets.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Cooling tower sump and pump piping design", "summary": "Place sump level above pump casing top for positive prime; pitch piping up to tower or pump suction to eliminate air pockets."}, "evidence": "98⏐ COMPONENTS
Design of piping from the tower sump to the pump should be
undertaken with caution. Place the sump level above the top of the
pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction
strainers should be equipped with inlet and outlet gauges that indi-
cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield
water velocities between 5 and 12 fps [1.5 and 3.7 m/s]. Use fric-
tion factors for aged or old pipes.

(net positive suction head—see Section 5.5.2) for the condenser-
water pump is provided, and design  the connections so that the flow
through each cooling tower has th e same pressure drop. Install

Design of piping from the tower sump to the pump should be
undertaken with caution. Place the sump level above the top of the
pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction

cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield

Design of piping from the tower sump to the pump should be
undertaken with caution. Place the sump level above the top of the
pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction
strainers should be equipped with inlet and outlet gauges that indi-
cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield

tion factors for aged or old pipes.

tower will fill and the other one will overflow.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_e9ba8e5fac18839a", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cooling tower winterization", "summary": "Winterize tower piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off; isolation valves must be in heated space.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Cooling tower winterization", "summary": "Winterize tower piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off; isolation valves must be in heated space."}, "evidence": "Winterize the tower
piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off.
In the latter case, the isolation va lves must be located in a heated
space. Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single
cell can be serviced while the others are active.
5.4.6 Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative condensers are used to  reject heat to the outdoor
air by means of the evaporation of water. Water is sprayed on the
condenser coils, and the resultant heat of vaporization, combined
with any sensible heat effect, co ols the liquid inside the coils. The
residual spray water is usually recirculated. An evaporative con-
denser uses much less makeup water than a cooling tower in a con-
ventional water-cooled condenser arrangement (approximately 1.6
to 2.0 gph/ton [0.5 to 0.6 mL/s pe r kW] by evaporation plus 0.5 to
0.6 gph/ton [0.15 to 0.18 mL/s pe r kW] for bleed water). Evapora-
tive condensers are used primarily  as refrigeration system condens-
ers.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_3c82743fd6bae08e", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Cooling tower cell isolation valving", "summary": "Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single cell can be serviced while others are active.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Cooling tower cell isolation valving", "summary": "Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single cell can be serviced while others are active."}, "evidence": "Winterize the tower
piping if necessary or provide for drain-down when pumps shut off.
In the latter case, the isolation va lves must be located in a heated
space. Provide valving or sump pump isolation so that any single
cell can be serviced while the others are active.
5.4.6 Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative condensers are used to  reject heat to the outdoor
air by means of the evaporation of water. Water is sprayed on the
condenser coils, and the resultant heat of vaporization, combined
with any sensible heat effect, co ols the liquid inside the coils. The
residual spray water is usually recirculated. An evaporative con-
denser uses much less makeup water than a cooling tower in a con-
ventional water-cooled condenser arrangement (approximately 1.6
to 2.0 gph/ton [0.5 to 0.6 mL/s pe r kW] by evaporation plus 0.5 to
0.6 gph/ton [0.15 to 0.18 mL/s pe r kW] for bleed water). Evapora-
tive condensers are used primarily  as refrigeration system condens-
ers.", "source_pages": [3], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_fa0c7b54785d851d", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Noise control initial step", "summary": "Establish noise criteria for all spaces and communicate to client early in design process.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Noise control initial step", "summary": "Establish noise criteria for all spaces and communicate to client early in design process."}, "evidence": "Even after a system has been selected, component
selection will significantly affect  system acoustical performance.
Noise can be transmitted to occupied spaces from central station
equipment along several airborne paths, through air or water flows,
along the walls of ducts or pipes,  or through the building structure.
If central station pumps or fans are used, each of these paths must
be analyzed and the transmission of sound and vibration reduced to
an acceptable level. Supply air outlets (and other terminal devices)
must be selected to provide ap propriate acoustical performance.
An initial step in noise control is to establish noise criteria for
all spaces. These criteria should be communicated to the client
early in the design process. All noise-generating sources within the
air-conditioning system must be identified.", "source_pages": [2], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_94d9dd6b1d600039", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Access for routine maintenance", "summary": "Ensure ready access for routine maintenance tasks such as changing air filters.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Access for routine maintenance", "summary": "Ensure ready access for routine maintenance tasks such as changing air filters."}, "evidence": "30⏐ THE DESIGN  PROCESS
 Ensure that there is ready acce ss for routine maintenance tasks,
such as changing air filters.
 Specify accessible service shutoff drain and vent valves for all
water coils and risers.
 Incorporate appropriate chemical treatment equipment for
open- and closed-loop water systems.
2.5.3 Noise and Vibration Control
Noise and vibration from HV AC&R equipment can be reduced
by proper selection of equipment, vibration control, and the interpo-
sition of sound attenuators and ba rriers. Residual sound may still be
objectionable, however, when e quipment is located near occupied
areas. HV AC&R and architectural de sign decisions must go hand-in-
hand since acoustical control may involve equipment location, floor
and wall assemblies, and room function.", "source_pages": [2], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_df26438ee3414857", "knowledge_type": "application_guidance", "title": "Service shutoff drain and vent valves", "summary": "Specify accessible service shutoff drain and vent valves for all water coils and risers.", "structured_payload": {"title": "Service shutoff drain and vent valves", "summary": "Specify accessible service shutoff drain and vent valves for all water coils and risers."}, "evidence": "30⏐ THE DESIGN  PROCESS

water coils and risers.
 Incorporate appropriate chemical treatment equipment for
open- and closed-loop water systems.

Design of piping from the tower sump to the pump should be
undertaken with caution. Place the sump level above the top of the
pump casing to provide positive pr ime. Pitch all piping up, either to
the tower or to the pump suction, to eliminate air pockets. Suction

cate when cleaning is required. Norm ally, piping is sized to yield
